name: Brooklyn Bridge
funFacts: P.T. Barnum paraded 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove to skeptical New Yorkers it wouldn't collapse
Emily Warren Roebling oversaw construction after her husband fell ill — she was the first person to cross the completed bridge
It was the first bridge to use steel wire for its cables and was the longest suspension bridge in the world for 20 years

name: Manhattan Bridge
funFacts: Carries more subway traffic than any other bridge in NYC — the B, D, N, and Q trains all cross it
The view of the Brooklyn Bridge perfectly framed through its stone arch from DUMBO is one of the most photographed spots in New York
It sways noticeably when multiple subway trains cross at once — the movement was so bad in the 1980s that two lanes were closed for a decade of repairs

name: Williamsburg Bridge
funFacts: When it opened it was the longest suspension bridge in the world, stealing the title from the Brooklyn Bridge
It triggered a massive migration of Jewish immigrants from the Lower East Side to Williamsburg — earning the nickname 'Jews' Highway'
The pedestrian walkway is split: north side for bikes, south side for pedestrians, with some of the best unobstructed skyline views of any NYC bridge

name: Queensboro Bridge (Ed Koch)
funFacts: F. Scott Fitzgerald immortalized it in The Great Gatsby — 'The city seen from the Queensboro Bridge is always the city seen for the first time'
Simon & Garfunkel wrote '59th Street Bridge Song (Feelin' Groovy)' about it
It's a cantilever bridge, not a suspension bridge — making it an engineering oddity among NYC's East River crossings

name: Roosevelt Island Bridge
funFacts: It's the only road connection to Roosevelt Island — everyone else takes the subway or the iconic aerial tramway
The island was called Welfare Island until 1973 and before that Blackwell's Island, home to a notorious prison, asylum, and smallpox hospital
At 0.1 miles it may be the shortest bridge walk in NYC but it connects to the 2-mile island promenade loop

name: High Bridge
funFacts: The oldest standing bridge in New York City — it was built as part of the Croton Aqueduct to carry drinking water to Manhattan
Closed to pedestrians for 45 years (1970–2015) due to crime concerns before a $61.8M restoration reopened it
The original stone arches over the river were replaced with a single steel arch in 1928 to improve ship navigation

name: Washington Bridge
funFacts: Not to be confused with the George Washington Bridge — this one is 42 years older and much less famous
When it opened, the New York Times called it 'the most beautiful bridge in the world' for its arched stone design
It's a double-decker: the upper level carries traffic while the lower level was originally designed for a never-built rail line

name: Alexander Hamilton Bridge
funFacts: Carries I-95 / Cross Bronx Expressway — one of the most congested highways in the United States
Named after Alexander Hamilton, who lived nearby at the Hamilton Grange, now a national memorial
Built as part of Robert Moses' highway network that controversially displaced thousands of Bronx residents

name: Macombs Dam Bridge
funFacts: Named after Robert Macomb, who built a dam across the Harlem River in 1813 — furious locals tore it down because it blocked boat traffic
It's a swing bridge that rotates on a central pivot to let boats pass through
Drops you right at Yankee Stadium — on game days this tiny bridge handles massive pedestrian crowds

name: 145th Street Bridge
funFacts: A swing bridge that pivots on its center pier — when it opens for boats, both halves of the roadway rotate 90 degrees
One of the busiest swing bridges remaining in NYC, opening multiple times a day for river traffic
It replaced an earlier wooden bridge from 1895 that was too narrow for the growing Bronx

name: Madison Avenue Bridge
funFacts: Despite the name, it doesn't connect to the Madison Avenue you're thinking of — the avenue ends miles south in Midtown
It's a swing bridge with a center pivot that still opens regularly for barge traffic on the Harlem River
The original 1884 bridge at this location was one of the very first crossings built over the Harlem River

name: Third Avenue Bridge
funFacts: The current bridge is a modern steel arch that replaced a swing bridge from 1898 — no more waiting for boats
One of the newest Harlem River crossings, designed to never need to open for marine traffic
The original 1898 bridge at this site was notorious for traffic jams when it swung open during rush hour

name: Willis Avenue Bridge
funFacts: The old 1901 swing bridge was floated 1.5 miles down the Harlem River on a barge to be demolished — a rare and dramatic engineering feat
The first new bridge built across the Harlem River in over 50 years when it opened in 2010
It was built slightly upstream of the old bridge so traffic could keep flowing during construction

name: University Heights Bridge
funFacts: This bridge was physically relocated — originally built at Fordham Road, it was disassembled and moved to W. 207th Street in 1908
One of the only bridges in NYC history to be picked up and moved to a completely different location
It's a swing bridge that still opens for boat traffic, pivoting on its center pier

name: Broadway Bridge
funFacts: Connects Manhattan to Marble Hill — a neighborhood that's geographically in the Bronx but politically part of Manhattan since 1914
Marble Hill became separated from Manhattan when the Harlem River Ship Canal was dug through in 1895, turning it into an island (later filled in)
The northernmost crossing of the Harlem River and the last bridge before the Hudson

name: George Washington Bridge
funFacts: The busiest motor vehicle bridge in the world — over 103 million vehicles cross it every year
The steel towers were designed to be clad in stone, but the Great Depression killed the budget — the exposed steel became its iconic look and people loved it
Le Corbusier called it 'the most beautiful bridge in the world' and 'the only seat of grace in the disordered city'

name: Pulaski Bridge
funFacts: A key crossing point for the NYC Marathon — runners cross from Brooklyn into Queens over this bridge at mile 13
Named after Casimir Pulaski, a Polish general who died fighting for American independence at the Siege of Savannah in 1779
During the 2003 Northeast blackout, this bridge became a critical pedestrian escape route when subways shut down

name: Greenpoint Avenue Bridge
funFacts: Crosses Newtown Creek, designated a federal Superfund site in 2010 — one of the most polluted waterways in America
It's a bascule (drawbridge) that lifts to let boats through the creek below
Beneath it lies the remnants of a massive underground oil spill — an estimated 17–30 million gallons of petroleum leaked into the soil over decades, rivaling the Exxon Valdez

name: Kosciuszko Bridge
funFacts: The original 1939 bridge was so steep that trucks regularly overheated climbing it — locals called it 'the Crack in the BQE'
Named after Tadeusz Kosciuszko, a Polish military engineer who designed fortifications for the Continental Army during the Revolution
The new cable-stayed bridge has a dedicated pedestrian/bike path with panoramic Manhattan skyline views that the original never had

name: RFK (Triborough) Bridge
funFacts: It's actually three separate bridges connected by viaducts on Randalls Island — linking Manhattan, Queens, and the Bronx in one complex
Robert Moses' crown jewel: it funded his empire of parks and highways through toll revenue for decades
Renamed for Robert F. Kennedy in 2008, but most New Yorkers still call it the Triborough

name: Cross Bay Veterans Memorial Bridge
funFacts: One of only two toll crossings entirely within NYC — the other is the nearby Marine Parkway Bridge
The original 1939 bridge was so corroded by Jamaica Bay's salt water and air that full replacement ($905M) was cheaper than repairs
Crosses Jamaica Bay Wildlife Refuge, one of the best birdwatching spots in NYC — you can watch JFK planes take off at eye level from the walkway
